coredata.dc:title: Surface coated fluorescent carbon nanoparticles/TiO2 as visible-light sensitive photocatalytic complexes for antifouling activity
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Young Kwang
Sharker, Shazid Md.
In, Insik
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: Surface protection of TiO2 photocatalysts by coating with fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (NPs) is a key factor in the photocatalyst design because it enhances the absorption of photons and promotes the decomposition of organic pollutants on various substrates. We report a synthesis of carbonized fluorescent particles by the acidic dehydration of catechol-q-poly (dimethyl aminoethyl methacrylate) (CA-PDMA) that improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under visible light irradiation. The carbonized fluorescent NPs (CA-FNPs) were efficiently combined with TiO2 through the reduction of intact catechol moieties (CA-FNPs/TiO2). This approach enhances the utilization of visible light in photocatalytic activity and suggests a new fluorescent coating platform. The coordination between carbonized CA-FNPs and TiO2 was confirmed by the time-dependent studies of methylene blue degradation under ultraviolet (UV) and visible light irradiation. Moreover, HeLa cell detachments from the CA-FNPs/TiO2-coated surface verify its antifouling properties in the presence of visible light. Protection of visible light-responsive photocatalysts from bio-fouling can thus enhance their efficiency and photocatalytic abilities as compared with traditional photocatalytic systems.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbonization of catechol-q-poly(dimethyl aminoethyl methacrylate) (CA-PDMA) by acid catalyst (CA-FNPs)
TiO2-integrated CA-FNPs produced by catechol chemistry (CA-FNPs/TiO2).

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Grant No. 10046506 and 10048377 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) , and Fusion Research R&D Program from the Korea Research Council for Industrial Science & Technology (No. G02054 ) and was also supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (No. 2014055946 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CA-PDMA, CA-FNPs, CA-PDMA/TiO2, CA-FNPs/TiO2 (Concentration: 0.05 mg/mL in DDW) and fluorescence emission spectra of (b) CA-PDMA/TiO2, (c) CA-FNPs and (d) CA-FNPs/TiO2 (Concentration: 5 mg/mL in DDW). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online).
The Raman D/G band of CA-PDMA, CA-FNPs and CA-FNPs/TiO2. (b) The TGA study reports of CA-PDMA and CA-FNPs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online).
The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of as-prepared (a) CA-FNPs and (b) CA-FNPs/TiO2 nanoparticles. The scale bars is 10 nm. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online).

The water static contact angle (°) measurements of non-carbonize (a) CA-PDMA/TiO2, and carbonized (b) CA-FNPs/TiO2 coated surface in response to UV and visible light irradiation. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online).

The core leveled XPS high resolution C 1s (a, a1), O 1s (b, b1), N 1s (c, c1), Ti 2p spectra of CA-FNPs and CA-FNPs/TiO2 nanostructure, respectively. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online).

SEM image of (a, a1) CA-PDMA, (b, b1) CA-PDMA/TiO2, (c, c1) CA-FNPs and (d, d1) CA- FNPs/TiO2 coated PET surface, respectively. The (a1), (b1), (c1), (d1) are the representative magnified image. The EDX spectra and related elemental composition of (e) CA-FNPs and (f) CA- FNPs/TiO2, respectively. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online).

The photocatalytic kinetics using (a) UV and (b) visible light on methylene blue (MB) in presence of CA-PDMA, CA-PDMA/TiO2, CA-FNPs and CA-FNPs/TiO2 on coated PET substrate. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online).
The confocal laser scan microscope (CLSM) image from CA-PDMA/TiO2 and CA- FNPs-TiO2 coated polypropylene surface. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online).

Surface coated antifouling study (a–b) on bare surface, CA-PDMA/TiO2, CA-FNPs/TiO2 coated surface. (b) The cells detachment study by using UV, and visible light irradiated CA-PDMA/TiO2, CA-FNPs/TiO2 coated PET substrate. The control group was used without irradiation. The HeLa cells seeding concentration was 1 × 105 cell/mL. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online).
Schematic illustration of energy position and visible-light photocatalytic mechanism of FNPs (CA-FNPs) integrated TiO2 NPs surface under visible-light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Surface coated fluorescent carbon nanoparticles/TiO2 as visible-light sensitive photocatalytic complexes for antifouling activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface protection of TiO2 photocatalysts by coating with fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (NPs) is a key factor in the photocatalyst design because it enhances the absorption of photons and promotes the decomposition of organic pollutants on various substrates. We report a synthesis of carbonized fluorescent particles by the acidic dehydration of catechol-q-poly (dimethyl aminoethyl methacrylate) (CA-PDMA) that improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under visible light irradiation. The carbonized fluorescent NPs (CA-FNPs) were efficiently combined with TiO2 through the reduction of intact catechol moieties (CA-FNPs/TiO2). This approach enhances the utilization of visible light in photocatalytic activity and suggests a new fluorescent coating platform. The coordination between carbonized CA-FNPs and TiO2 was confirmed by the time-dependent studies of methylene blue degradation under ultraviolet (UV) and visible light irradiation. Moreover, HeLa cell detachments from the CA-FNPs/TiO2-coated surface verify its antifouling properties in the presence of visible light. Protection of visible light-responsive photocatalysts from bio-fouling can thus enhance their efficiency and photocatalytic abilities as compared with traditional photocatalytic systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy has attracted significant attention in the past decades as a sustainable, nonhazardous, and economic method of solving the energy crisis and decreasing environmental pollution [1]. The field of photo-electrochemical solar energy conversion significantly overlaps with the area of environmental photocatalysis, which requires not only self-cleaning surfaces, but also antifouling ones [2]. Fouling is a phenomenon that occurs when aquatic microorganisms bind to a surface and form a conditioning layer providing an accessible platform for other aquatic species, such as diatoms and algae, to attach, proliferate, and finally degrade it [3]. Different semiconductor photocatalysts (TiO2, ZnO, ZnS, CdS etc.) have been extensively studied for this purpose [4,5], and titanium dioxide (TiO2) was found to be the most promising candidate for large scale industrial applications [6].

TiO2 is a relatively inexpensive, environmental friendly, and chemically inert semiconductor material with a band gap of ∼3.2 eV (corresponding to a wavelength of ∼390 nm). In the past 20 years, TiO2 has been continuously utilized in solar cells, environmental reformation, sensing, coatings, and more recently, in various photocatalytic materials [7]. Although photocatalysis can occur on the TiO2 surface, the wide band gap energy and high recombination rate limits its possible photocatalytic applications. Various attempts were made to overcome these limitations including element doping, sensitization with a visible band gap semiconductor, and the use of traditional quantum dots (QDs) [6,8]. However, the inherent toxicity of the QD core (e.g., cadmium, selenium) may render the nanoparticles toxic to both cell cultures and live animals [9]. Carbon-based FNPs or so-called carbon dots (CDs) have recently attracted growing attention due to their unique properties including chemical stability, biocompatibility, and high optical absorption efficiency with tunable emission. The distinctive optical absorptivity of FNPs makes them promising candidates for various applications in the fields of bioimaging, medical diagnosis, catalysis, and photovoltaic devices [10,11]. While the exact chemical nature of FNPs is still unclear, the synergistic effect of their sp2/sp3 carbon core and the surface/molecule state contributes to their optoelectronic properties. In addition, the recombination of photo-excited electron–hole (e−/h+) pairs of FNPs enables the creation of a corresponding energy band that depends on the band gap size and edge functionalization while the band gap of FNPs is tuned to the lowest possible frequencies to harvest visible light [12,13]. As a result, FNPs promise significantly to enhance the light utilization, and their relative band edge position permits the transfer of electrons from the surface.

Considering the photocatalytic properties of TiO2 and the photo-electronic properties of CDs similar to those of FNPs with tunable energy bands, the combination of both species can produce a unique material that overcomes the existing limitations of the TiO2-based photocatalysts. Moreover, a photocatalytic surface protected from contact with atmospheric biofoulants can possibly overcome the limitations of all currently existing photocatalytic systems. Thus, we report a creation of visible-light sensitized FNPs by the controlled carbonization reaction of catechol-q-poly (dimethyl aminoethyl methacrylate) (CA-PDMA) by acidic dehydration synthesis. The as-obtained CA-FNPs retain their surface coat ability due to the residual moieties of adhesive catechol. It was found that the CA-FNPs coating on the TiO2 surface possesses antifouling properties with improved light absorption abilities, is active under both UV and visible light irradiation, and can significantly enhance the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2–based systems.

Trizma base, Trizma HCl, 2-Chloro-3′,4′-dihydroxyacetophenone (CA), dimethyl aminoethyl methacrylate (DMA), 2,4-dinitrotoluene, N,N-diethylaniline, toluene, methanol and concentrated H2SO4 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Korea. Penicillin-streptomycin, fetal bovine serum (FBS), 0.25% (w/v) trypsin, 0.05% (w/v) EDTA solution, and RPMI-1640 medium were obtained from Gibco BRL (Carlsbad, CA, USA). The CA-PDMA (Mn: 14,050) was synthesized using the procedure described in our previous report [14].

The 1H NMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker Advance 400 MHz spectrometer with deuterium oxide (D2O) as solvent. The UV–vis spectra were produced using an Optizen 2020UV spectrophotometer (Mecasys Co, Korea). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were obtained with an Omicrometer ESCALAB (Omicrometer, Taunusstein, Germany), and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a L550B luminescence spectrometer from Perkin Elmer. Static water contact angles were measured with a DO3210 instrument (KRUSS Ltd., Germany), SEM (JSM-6700F Jeol, Japan) and EDX (Inca X-sight 7421 Oxford, UK). Using an infrared camera (NEC Avio, Thermo Tracer TH9100) and transmission electron microscopy (FEI, Netherlands). The Raman spectra were obtained using a laser Raman spectrophotometer (NRS-3200 Jasco, Japan). Energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX) was used to determine the elemental composition of individual particles. EDX spectra obtained from individual particles were analyzed using a vector-based algorithm to determine the relative abundance of 3 elements: carbon (C), titanium (Ti), oxygen (O) containing particles were defined as those with a K-α peak at 6.4 keV, representing greater than 2% of the quantified elemental composition. Elemental mapping was used to confirm the three elements of carbon (C), titanium (Ti), oxygen (O) contains in products. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a Nano LED laser light source (Horiba Jobin Yvon NanoLog spectrophotometer) at 375 nm for the excitation, and the data were fitted by a multi-exponential decay model. The samples for the fluorescence lifetime measurements were prepared by dissolving CA-FNPs and CA-FNPs/TiO2 in an aqueous solution (5.0 mg/mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbonization of catechol-q-poly(dimethyl aminoethyl methacrylate) (CA-PDMA) by acid catalyst (CA-FNPs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis method was adopted from the procedure reported elsewhere [13]. Briefly, 1 g of CA-PDMA was dissolve in 10 mL of 36 N H2SO4 and allowed to react for 10 min at room temperature. Finally, the mixture was purify by dialysis (with a molecular weight cutoff equal to 3500) against water and freeze-dried to obtain the final product (CA-FNPs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TiO2-integrated CA-FNPs produced by catechol chemistry (CA-FNPs/TiO2).
ce:section.ce:para: 1 g of the obtained CA-FNPs and TiO2 (0.01 g) were dissolved in 20 mL of Tris buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 8.5) and stirred at room temperature for 12 h. At the end of the reaction, the solution was centrifuge at 4000 rpm for 10 min to collect the CA-FNPs/TiO2 product.

To investigate the antifouling properties of the synthesized CA-FNPs/TiO2 material, HeLa and MDCK cells were culture in a 6-well plate. A polymer-coated PET substrate was immersed in 2 mL of the phosphate buffer solution (PBS) followed by the addition of 2 mL of the cell medium. The substrate was then incubating for 1 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator. After that, the medium was removing and 2 mL of the cell solution (with a concentration of 1 × 106 cells/mL) was applied to the substrate, which was subsequently transferred back into the incubator for another 24 h. Finally, the media was removed, and the substrate was washed with PBS several times. The cell fixation on the substrate was performed by using 4% formaldehyde solution followed by its incubation for another 1 h. The non-adhered cells were removed by suction, after which the substrate was washed again with PBS, and the total number of adhered cells was counted.

Carbon-derived FNPs have received significant attention because of their multiphasic properties distinguishing them from other nanostructures [10]. Recently, graphene-derived FNPs have shown to improve the photocatalytic behavior of TiO2 due to their advantageous up conversion fluorescence and electron transfer properties of the carbon core [15]. These results attribute the improvement in photocatalytic performance of the FNPs to the favorable electron transfer.

In general, TiO2 NPs become excited under UV light to produce electron–hole pairs. Previous literature suggests that the FNPs can absorb both visible and near-infrared light, which can be converted to shorter wavelengths due to their up-conversion properties [11]. As a result, FNPs introduced on the surface of the TiO2 NPs system can increase the number of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. Scheme 1 shows the visible-light photocatalytic mechanism of energy conversion by the FNPs (CA-FNPs) integrated TiO2.

Fig. 1 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the catechol-derived polymer (CA-PDMA), as-prepared FNPs (CA-FNPs), and TiO2-integrated FNPs (CA-FNPs/TiO2). The absorption band at about 280 nm corresponds to integrated catechol groups, while the absorption edge extending from the UV into visible region (400–500 nm) represents the band energy of the FNPs-modified TiO2 [14–17]. As shown in Fig. 1c, the CA-FNPs exhibited the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission band, a prominent feature of carbon-derived FNPs. After the incorporation of TiO2, the resulting composites (CA-FNPs/TiO2) showed the absence of the emission band in the visible region between 400 and 800 nm (Fig. 1b) [18]. However, applying the as-prepared CA-FNPs to the TiO2 NPs led to enhanced visible-light excited emission, illustrating that the FNPs/TiO2 absorption band extends from the UV into the visible region (Fig. 1d). Moreover, the 1H NMR spectra showed an aromatic proton peak at 6.5–7.5 ppm corresponding to the catechol species in the CA-PDMA polymer composite (Fig. S1) [19].

An apparent G band at 1590 cm−1 and a D band at 1360 cm−1 were observed in the Raman spectra for both the CA-PDMA and CA-FNPs/TiO2 species (Fig. 2 a) indicating the presence of the sp2 with sp3 hybrid carbons [20]. However, distinguish of such a D/G band have found to invisible from native CA-PDMA precursor. The intensity ratio [ID/IG], which is often used to evaluate the structural content of carbonized nanocrystals, also indicates different sp2/sp3 ratios for carbonized FNPs. Moreover, the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) identified the decomposition products of carbonized CA-FNPs resulting from the acidic dehydration of its source material, CA-PDMA (Fig. 2b).

The acidic dehydration of the composite material can possibly produce tiny fragments of carbon derivatives resulting in the formation of FNPs, which retain some residual moieties from the precursor material [20]. Using the catechol-derived CA-PDMA composite material as precursor and concentrated H2SO4 as dehydration media, carbon-derived FNPs were synthesized, indicating the formation of graphene-like carbonized materials with the same lattice d-space values of 0.33–0.35 nm (Fig. 3 a). The TiO2 NPs coated with the CA-FNPs nanoparticles (CA-FNPs/TiO2 NPs) had different d-spacing values in the region of 0.46–0.50 nm (Fig. 3a) [13,18]. Since CA-FNPs/TiO2 NPs were produced by the catechol reduction of CA-FNPs, it may be expected that the product would contain a mixture of FNPs and TiO2 with different d-spacing values, symmetries, and defects. Furthermore, the particle sizes (diameters) and height profiles for both CA-FNPs and CA-FNPs/TiO2 were evaluated by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The obtained depth magnitudes of around 34.5 nm are consistent with the formation of TiO2-supported CA-FNPs (Fig. S2).

Absorption of photo energy requires a surface that is capable of converting the maximum amount of the incident light energy. To examine the effect of surface coating on the photocatalyst performance, both the non-carbonized CA-PDMA/TiO2 and carbonized CA-FNPs/TiO2 coatings were applied on polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polypropylene (PP), polystyrene (PS), polyvinyl chloride (PVC), and silicon (Si) substrates. As shown in Fig. 4 , the application of CA-PDMA/TiO2 and CA-FNPs/TiO2 coatings on the PET, PP, PS, and PVC substrates led to a decrease in hydrophobicity, confirmed by the decrease in the water contact angle. At the same time, the Si substrates showed an increase in water contact angle from 36° to 43° because the bare Si surface is more hydrophilic than the CA-PDMA/TiO2 and CA-FNPs/TiO2 coatings [21]. In contrast to these data, both the CA-PDMA/TiO2 and CA-FNPs/TiO2 coatings showed substantially low contact angles under UV irradiation, which can be explained by the generation of reactive oxygen and hydroxide species in the course of a TiO2-mediated photochemical reaction, causing the decrease in the observed contact angle due to increased hydrophilicity (Fig. 4) [7,22]. However, the situation becomes different under visible light irradiation. The CA-PDMA/TiO2-coated substrate did not show any changes under visible light (Fig. 4a), while the CA-FNPs/TiO2-coated substrate exhibited lower values of contact angles, similar to the UV light-treated system (Fig. 4b). It can be suggested that the carbon structures attached to TiO2 possess excellent upconversion properties originating from the quantum confinement effect, thus enabling utilization of the full spectrum of sunlight for photocatalysis [23].

CA-FNPs/TiO2 NPs are formed by a reaction between the catechol groups of CA-FNPs and TiO2 under alkaline conditions (pH = 8.5), resulting in the detection of both carbon species and TiO2 NPs on the surface. The carbonized FNPs (CA-FNPs) and photocatalyst FNPs (CA-FNPs/TiO2) were characterized by XPS (Fig. 5 ) [12]. A typical XPS spectrum indicated the following binding energies corresponding to the C1s signal: a strong peak for CC at 284.2 eV, C–C at 285 eV, C-N at 285.4 eV, and CO at 288 eV and a prominent O-CO peak at 286.9 eV (Figs. 5a–a1). The peaks at 531.1 and 533.1 eV of the O 1s spectra corresponded to the CA-FNP species (Fig. 5b), while the peak centered at 529.2 eV represented the Ti-O binding energy (Fig. 5b1). A peak at 399.2 eV in the N 1s range corresponded to the N-C bonding of the carbonized CA-FNPs (Figs. 5c–c1). The binding energy peaks at 458.3 and 464.1 eV of the core Ti 2p level corresponded to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 lines, respectively (Fig. 5d1) [24]. These peaks were absent in the CA-FNPs XPS spectrum due to the absence of TiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 5d). Hence, the overall XPS studies demonstrated the structural integrity of the CA-FNPs and CA-FNPs/TiO2 species.

Carbonized coatable FNPs were obtained by controlling dehydration of CA-PDMA in the presence of concentrated H2SO4. Fig. 6 shows the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the surface-coated CA-PDMA (Fig. 6a–a1) and TiO2-immobilized CA-PDMA (Fig. 6b–b1). The corresponding morphology studies of the carbonized FNPs (CA-FNPs) and FNPs on the TiO2 (CA-FNPs/TiO2) showed the same surface orientation (similar to other composite materials). However, the magnified SEM images revealed morphological differences between the CA-PDMA/TiO2 and CA-FNPs/TiO2 coated surfaces, indicating adhesive catechol-guided integrated nanoparticles (Fig. 6d–d1) [25]. The surface oriented TiO2 within CA-PDMA and CA-FNPs can be clearly distinguished because carbonized FNPs have different morphology than polymer composite (CA-PDMA). The surface of nanoparticles changed with the deposited TiO2 in the synthesis process, which is most likely due to the adhesion of TiO2 induced by the presence of catechol. A number of TiO2 nanoparticles were oriented on the surface, which will be of prominent importance for light harvesting and a higher photochemical conversion. The composite structure was further characterized by energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 6e, both the CA-FNPs and the CA-FNPs/TiO2 composites exhibited the similar carbon levels (in wt. %). For the CA-FNPs/TiO2 species (Fig. 6f), the EDX spectra showed an increase in the oxygen (O) level peak intensity and the appearance of a new peak corresponding to Ti species (2.09 wt. %). However, EDX may have some overlapping peaks and the accuracy in inhomogeneous are therefore are rectified in combination with XPS measurements [26]. The obtained EDX data are consistent with the previous SEM results and, similar to XPS base elemental composite ratio (Table S1), further demonstrated the integrity of the CA-FNPs/TiO2 NPs [27]. Moreover, the elemental mapping of carbon (C), oxygen (O) and titanium (Ti) have confirmed the elemental components of CA-FNPs/TiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. S3).

In addition to the contact angle measurements, the fluorescence properties of the coated surfaces were evaluated by using a confocal laser scan microscope (CLSM) [10]. Depending on the excitation laser wavelength (405, 488 or 543 nm), the fluorescence emission shows different color (blue, green or red). The light emission observed also noticeably increased for the CA-FNPs/TiO2-coated substrate compared with its precursor, the CA-PDMA coating (Fig. 8 ) [12]. These results may lead to the possibility of drawing fluorescence images on the versatile substrates. Because the direction of charge transfer can be examined by monitoring of fluorescence decay time of CA-FNPs before and after the hybridization with TiO2 nanoparticles, the fluorescence lifetimes of both have been measured and the log plots are compared in Fig. S6. From Fig. S6, CA-FNPs/TiO2 showed slower fluorescence decay time (τ 2 = 2.56 ns) compared with pristine CA-FNPs (τ 1 = 1.99 ns), which means the charge transfer from TiO2 nanoparticles to CA-FNPs [33,34]. Through the electron transfer from the conduction band of TiO2 nanoparticles to the excited state of CA-FNPs, CA-FNPs can be able to show higher fluorescence intensity and much slower fluorescence decay time, probably through the dominating electron accepting tendency of CA-FNPs was used in this study.

Bio-fouling limits the performance of applicable surfaces [3]. Catechol-conjugated polymers can potentially hold antifouling materials on a substrate surface for a prolonged time. The residual catechols detected on carbonized CA-PDMA-coated substrates were efficiently used in the antifouling studies. The as-prepared CA-PDMA/TiO2- and CA-FNPs/TiO2-coated surfaces were evaluated by quantification of nonspecific cell adhesion and the electron microscopy imaging technique. In a typical antifouling experiment, the adherent HeLa endothelial cells (a cervical cancer cell line) were used to measure the performance of the anti-adhesion coatings [35,36]. The prevention of non-specific cells adhesion in an adherent cells culture provides reliable indicator for antifouling coatings. HeLa cells were cultured according to a previously reported method and observed using optical microscopy imaging (Fig. 9 ) [36]. A detailed imaging description of the cell attachment is presented in Fig. 9 and displays ∼90% cell fouling resistance of the CA-PDMA/TiO2 coated substrates (detachment) exposed to UV light [37,38]. However, unlike the CA-PDMA/TiO2 coatings, the carbonized CA-FNPs/TiO2 completely prevented cell adhesion (antifouling) under both UV and visible light exposure. The results presented may lead to a new possibility of using sunlight for antifouling applications. The property of non-specific cells adhesion were also rectified against another adherent mammalian MDCK cells that reasonably support the above finding (Fig. S7).

Typically in all cases, the narrow band gap of FNPs was associated with the absorption of UV as well as visible light of the solar spectrum needed to recombination with large band gap TiO2 to improve its photocatalytic activity [11,12]. These findings extend those of recent reports, confirming that depositing TiO2 on CDs tending to visible light harvesting and upconversion for improving photocatalysis [11,12,17,24,39]. However, these studies have either been visible light photocatalysis or have not focused on coating ability whose important was photocatalytic surface protector. In this study, we tested the photocatalytic kinetics to which an extended visible light photocatalysis improved coating ability being treating for environmental bio-fouling protection such as antifouling effect. Our results provide compelling evidence for antifouling coating nanoparticles development with such CA-FNPs and suggest that this approach could be sustainable to utilize solar energy in photocatalysis applications.

In order to achieve the maximum desirable effect, a photocatalytic technology inherently requires the ability to absorb in the visible light region and the antifouling properties of a material when used outdoors. Our studies were focused on the photoelectronic properties of the FNPs and were motivated by the idea of using visible light energy for TiO2 photocatalysis. Similar to other carbonized materials, FNPs exhibit broad UV–vis absorption characteristics, and the presence of catechol group allows FNPs to be bonded to the TiO2 surfaces in a facile way. These combinations showed identical lattice d-spacings in the TEM images, while the XPS studies clearly indicated structural composites. The adhesive surface coatability of the FNPs was confirmed by the contact angle, SEM, and EDX measurements. Moreover, the photocatalytic performance of the studied materials was investigated for the solutions and coatings showing that the visible light-irradiated CA-FNPs/TiO2 composite can effectively utilize solar energy in photocatalysis applications. In addition, using antifouling properties of the nanocomposites in visible light-mediated photocatalysis can overcome the existing limitations of the currently used materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Grant No. 10046506 and 10048377 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE), and Fusion Research R&D Program from the Korea Research Council for Industrial Science & Technology (No. G02054) and was also supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (No. 2014055946).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.03.036.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Titanium dioxide photocatalysis in atmospheric chemistry
